original_abstract:
To achieve global net-zero emissions by 2050, hydrogen produced via water electrolysis—specifically "green hydrogen"—must scale from current sub-gigawatt levels to over 1,000 GW within the next quarter-century. Proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) are uniquely positioned to lead this transition due to their high efficiency and dynamic response; however, substantial hurdles in cost, durability, and material scarcity persist. 

This review provides a comprehensive technological roadmap for the cell-level advancements required for gigawatt-scale PEMWE deployment. We analyze the global policy landscape and evaluate the economic drivers necessary to reduce hydrogen production costs toward the U.S. Department of Energy’s goal of $1/kg. The discussion details critical improvements for individual cell components: optimizing low-iridium-loading anode catalyst layers, developing reinforced and thinner membranes to mitigate gas crossover, and engineering porous transport layers (PTLs) with microporous interfaces to enhance catalyst utilization. 

Special emphasis is placed on establishing standardized accelerated stress tests (ASTs) to predict system lifetimes and understand degradation under the fluctuating loads of renewable energy coupling. Supplemented by strategic perspectives from leading hydrogen technology companies (Nel Hydrogen, Plug Power, and others), the paper addresses supply chain vulnerabilities—particularly regarding iridium and perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) materials—as well as safety regulations and manufacturing scalability. Ultimately, this work harmonizes scientific research goals with industrial requirements to facilitate the rapid expansion of the green hydrogen economy.
humanized_abstract:
The world’s hydrogen production from water by electrolysis must rise from a combination of the low single-digit gigawatts from today of all known green hydrogen production from water—through electrolysis—to more than a megawatt gigawatts by 2025, if we are to achieve the worldwide goal of net-zero carbon emissions by 2050. Proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) will be the hydrogen production technology of choice. Today’s obstacles to this technology must be mitigated and will include lowering costs and increasing durability and the availability of membranes and other materials needed for mass production.

Fuel cell materials for megawatt scale power generation – roadmap to gigawatt scale PEMWE via cell-level innovation This review provides a technology roadmap for the advancements required at a cell level to enable reliable and cost effective gigawatt scale power generation using PEMWE technology. An overview of the current energy and climate change policy backdrop is given and the economic drivers are discussed to evaluate how hydrogen production costs may be lowered to achieve DOE’s target of $1/kg. The need and extent of improvements required in individual cell components are investigated, including performance gains from low-iridium-loading anode catalyst layers, less gas crossover achieved with the use of reinforced and thinner membranes, as well as improvements in PTL performance with microporous interfaces.

We focus on developing standardized accelerated stress tests (ASTs) in order to estimate the lifespan of a system and to evaluate the degradation of the components under variable renewable energy as well as transient current fluctuations. Supported by strategic notes from leading hydrogen technology companies (Nel Hydrogen, Plug Power, …), we address the current challenges in the supply chain related to iridium and perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) materials, as well as safety issues related to the scaling-up of production. This work therefore aims at synchronizing the research orientations and needs at the scientific level and the industrial level in order to enable the rapid development of the green hydrogen economy.
